query: use of methylergometrine, oxytocin, misoprostol, and carboprost in postpartum hemorrhage

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Prehospital Deliveries -- Complications -- Postpartum Hemorrhage
retrieved_docs.content: In a hospital setting, administering oxytocin immediately following the delivery of the infant is the most critical intervention for reducing the risk of PPH. For this reason, ACOG, the World Health Organization, and the American Academy of Family Physicians all recommend the universal administration of a uterotonic agent, usually oxytocin, following all births by obstetric clinicians. [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Vaginal Bleeding -- Treatment / Management -- Obstetric Bleeding
retrieved_docs.content: Postpartum hemorrhage should be treated with uterotonic agents, including oxytocin, methylergonovine, 15-methyl prostaglandin F 2α , and/or misoprostol. [7] Lacerations (including cervical lacerations) should be sutured. Bimanual uterine massage can help stimulate an atonic uterus to contract while uterotonic agents are administered. This is done by placing one hand inside the vagina, beneath the cervix, and the other hand on the abdomen; the uterus is then compressed and massaged between the two hands. If this is unsuccessful, a uterine tamponade balloon or thrombin-soaked gauze can be placed in the uterus, applying compressive pressure within the uterus. If bleeding persists, surgery is indicated; procedures include vascular ligation, uterine compression sutures, and hysterectomy. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostaglandins -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: When misoprostol is used for labor induction, monitoring should include the onset and progression of uterine contractions and cervical ripening. [39] When used for postpartum hemorrhage, monitoring should focus on excessive vaginal or uterine bleeding. With carboprost therapy, monitoring should include uterine tone and bleeding control. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pregnancy Contractions -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Understanding the physiology of uterine contractions allows clinicians to use targeted therapy for both the induction and cessation of labor. Commonly used medications for induction of labor include oxytocin, misoprostol, and dinoprostone. Because uterine contractions dually function to minimize postpartum bleeding, these are the same medications used to manage postpartum hemorrhage. Carboprost, an analog of PGF2a, is an additional medication used to manage postpartum hemorrhage, not used for the induction of labor.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Treatment / Management -- Uterine Atony Management
retrieved_docs.content: Carboprost : As a 15-methyl prostaglandin F2-α analog, carboprost acts on prostaglandin receptors to stimulate uterine contractions. The recommended dosage is 250 μg IM or intramyometrially every 15 to 90 minutes for a maximum of 8 doses, with peak serum concentrations reached in approximately 15 minutes. Carboprost is contraindicated in severe hepatic, renal, and cardiovascular disease and may cause bronchospasm in patients with asthma.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: The recommended strategy to manage PPH is preventing hemorrhage from beginning or worsening. The International Federation of Gynecology and Obstetrics recommends the following anticipatory interventions to avoid PPH in all deliveries: Oxytocin 10 IU IV/IM for all vaginal and Cesarean deliveries during or after placental delivery. If oxytocin is unavailable, other uterotonics (eg, ergometrine or methylergometrine) may be used if not contraindicated. Early uterine tone assessment immediately following delivery in all women to identify uterine atony. Trained clinicians may employ controlled cord traction during placental delivery to reduce blood loss; if skilled birth attendants are unavailable, controlled cord traction is not recommended due to the risk of uterine inversion. In women who have received prophylactic oxytocin, sustained uterine massage is not recommended to prevent PPH.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostaglandins -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Misoprostol's mechanism of action has led to several off-label uses in obstetrics and gynecology. [25] The drug selectively binds to EP2 and EP3 receptors, leading to uterine muscle contractions. As a result, it is used for cervical ripening and labor induction. [25] As misoprostol induces uterine contractions, it is also used for the termination of intrauterine pregnancy in the first trimester and for the treatment of postpartum hemorrhage. [26] [14] Carboprost, a PGF2α analog, has potent uterotonic effects, compresses blood vessels, and stops bleeding. [6] Dinoprostone, a PGE2 analog, stimulates the release of PGF2α, thereby increasing the sensitivity of uterine muscles to both endogenous and exogenous oxytocin. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Management of Ex Utero Intrapartum Treatment (EXIT) and Fetal Surgery -- Clinical Significance -- EXIT Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: EXIT procedures are like midgestational procedures except that the umbilical cord is clamped, and the fetus is delivered at the end of the procedure. So, they need profound uterine relaxation during the procedure but needs adequate uterine tone immediately after clamping the umbilical cord to minimize postpartum hemorrhage. Lengthy hysterotomy and profound uterine relaxation may put the mother at significant risk of bleeding. These procedures are associated with a higher incidence of transfusion compared to a conventional cesarean section (6% vs. 2% to 4%). [15] So adequate uterine relaxation is provided with short-acting uterine relaxants such as volatile anesthetics and nitroglycerine while minimizing the use of longer-acting tocolytics. After clamping the cord, Oxytocin is usually initiated as a bolus and continued as an infusion. Methylergometrine and Carboprost may be needed to gain adequate uterine tone. Fetus monitoring, analgesia, and anesthesia are similar to mid gestational open procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Treatment / Management -- Uterine Atony Management
retrieved_docs.content: Misoprostol : Misoprostol is a prostaglandin E1 analog with a more prolonged onset of action than other uterotonics, depending on the administration route, which includes oral, sublingual, rectal, or buccal routes. The analog should be avoided in patients with anticoagulant therapy or cardiovascular disease; adverse effects may include nausea, diarrhea, and fever.

retrieved_docs.title: Misoprostol -- Administration -- Adult Dosage
retrieved_docs.content: Postpartum hemorrhage: Misoprostol is commonly used in combination with oxytocin. However, misoprostol monotherapy may be considered for postpartum hemorrhage if oxytocin is unavailable. The recommended dose is 600 to 1000 mcg as a single dose, administered orally, sublingually, or rectally for the treatment of postpartum hemorrhage. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Treatment / Management -- Uterine Atony Management
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytocin : The International Federation of Gynecology and Obstetrics recommends giving oxytocin 10 international units (IU) IV or intramuscularly (IM) first-line for uterine atony if not administered prophylactically. The oxytocin hormone is naturally produced by the posterior pituitary and works rapidly, with an onset of action within 1 to 6 minutes, to cause uterine contraction following IV administration. Oxytocin has minimal adverse events and may be given during bimanual massage in response to hemorrhage. Methylergonovine : Ergot alkaloids (eg, ergometrine, ergonovine, and methylergonovine) are serotonergic receptor agonists and partial α-adrenergic receptor agonists that cause sustained uterine contractions. The onset of action is approximately 1 to 3 minutes. Methylergonovine 200 μg IM or IV is typically recommended but is relatively contraindicated in patients with hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: Misoprostol -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Misoprostol is used off-label for second-trimester pregnancy termination, either as monotherapy or in combination with intramuscular methotrexate. [1] This drug is also used in the expectant management of missed and incomplete abortions. At lower doses, misoprostol is effective for cervical ripening and inducing labor in full-term pregnancies, as well as for inducing labor following intrauterine fetal demise. [10] In addition, misoprostol is used to treat postpartum hemorrhage when uterine massage fails and other uterotonic medications are not readily available. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Uterine Atony -- Treatment / Management -- Initial Medical Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytocin (Pitocin) can be given IV 10 to 40 units per 1000 ml or 10 units intramuscularly (IM). The rapid undiluted infusion may cause hypotension. Methylergonovine (Methergine) given IM 0.2 mg. Given every 2 to 4 hours. Should be avoided in patients with hypertension. 15-methyl-PGF2-alpha (Hemabate) given IM 0.25 mg. Given every 15 to 90 minutes for a maximum of 8 doses. Should be avoided in asthmatics. May cause diarrhea, fevers, or tachycardia. It is expensive. Misoprostol (Cytotec): 800 to 1000 mg placed rectally. May cause a low-grade fever. It has a delayed action. Dinoprostone (Prostin E2) 20 mg vaginal or rectal suppository may be given every 2 hours.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostaglandins -- Indications -- Off-Label Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Misoprostol is used off-label for cervical ripening, labor induction, pregnancy termination, and treatment of postpartum hemorrhage. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Multiple Birth Delivery -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: A postpartum hemorrhage kit should be available in the room before delivery. This kit should include misoprostol 200 mcg tablets, oxytocin 10 U, and methylergonovine 0.2 mg, and 15-methyl PGF 2α 0.25mg ampules. Access to an intrauterine balloon device, such as the Bakri balloon or the Jada device, should be secured.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pregnancy Contractions -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytocin is commonly used in modern obstetric practice to increase contractions when the labor process has failed to promote vaginal delivery. [23] Prostaglandins are active contractile agents, and their analogs are combined with mifepristone (an antiprogestin)for uterine evacuation during early and second-trimester pregnancy.[24][25][24] Misoprostol is cheaper than gemeprost and does not need refrigeration, so it is the prostaglandin of choice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2713
retrieved_docs.content: Dinoprost is classified as a member of the prostaglandin oxytocic group, a class of agents that act as uterotonic drugs to stimulate uterine smooth‑muscle contraction. This categorization indicates that dinoprost shares the pharmacologic properties of prostaglandin oxytocics, which are commonly used to induce labor, augment uterine activity during delivery, and manage postpartum hemorrhage. Understanding dinoprost’s membership in this drug class helps clinicians anticipate its mechanism of action, therapeutic indications, and potential side‑effects related to uterine stimulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2337
retrieved_docs.content: Dinoproston, also known as prostaglandin E₂, is classified as a member of the prostaglandin oxytocics group. Prostaglandin oxytocics are a class of prostaglandin compounds that stimulate uterine contractions and are commonly employed in obstetric practice for cervical ripening, induction of labor, and medical termination of pregnancy. By belonging to this class, dinoproston shares these pharmacologic properties and is used clinically to facilitate labor and manage certain gynecologic conditions, guiding clinicians in the selection and expected effects of uterotonic therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1891
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytocin, a peptide hormone released from the posterior pituitary gland, has a direct physiological relationship with the uterus; it binds to oxytocin receptors on uterine smooth‑muscle cells, triggering strong contractions that are essential for the initiation and progression of labor as well as for postpartum uterine involution. This interaction underlies the clinical use of oxytocin and its analogs to induce or augment labor, to control postpartum hemorrhage, and to support obstetric management, highlighting the hormone’s pivotal role in regulating uterine activity during pregnancy and after delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 734
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother is a broad category of maternal complications that encompasses several specific conditions. It has as child entities postpartum hemorrhage (نزف ما بعد الولادة), failure to induce labor (فشل تحريض المخاض), and fetal death (موت الجنين). In addition, this category is classified as obstetric cervical tear (تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم) and pelvic obstetric hematoma (ورم دموي توليدي حوضي). Together, these interrelated complications illustrate the diverse and serious events that can arise during the postpartum period and influence the clinical management of the mother.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2324
retrieved_docs.content: Induction by cervical dilation and other procedures to assist delivery are closely related interventions used to facilitate childbirth, and they may be considered synonymous in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1021
retrieved_docs.content: organizes general obstetric procedure categories (إجراء توليدي, Antepartum Procedures, Obstetrics incision) and specific clinical actions—ranging from diagnostic sampling and membrane management to fetal stimulation, reduction of multiple gestations, and surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancies—providing a comprehensive framework for coordinating obstetric care across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and postpartum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 109
retrieved_docs.content: disorders, and obstetric interventions together form a comprehensive framework for risk assessment, care planning, and management of maternal‑fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2323
retrieved_docs.content: Medical or surgical induction of labor is a clinical approach employed to initiate uterine contractions and facilitate delivery when spontaneous labor does not occur or when early delivery is medically indicated. One specific technique that falls under this category is artificial rupture of membranes, referred to in Arabic as تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي. This procedure involves deliberately breaking the amniotic sac to release fetal fluid, thereby stimulating prostaglandin release and enhancing uterine activity. By incorporating تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي as a component of medical or surgical induction of labor, clinicians can more effectively manage the timing of birth and improve outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2791
retrieved_docs.content: Episiotomy and تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي (artificial membrane rupture) are both classified under الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالتوليد (obstetric therapeutic procedures). By recognizing these interventions as subcomponents of the broader category of obstetric therapeutic care, clinicians can better organize treatment options, understand their related indications, and apply appropriate management strategies within the field of obstetrics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2843
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical induction of labor is classified as a specific type of induction and delivery procedure, meaning it falls under the broader category of interventions used to initiate and manage childbirth. Recognizing surgical induction of labor as a subset of induction and delivery procedures helps clinicians organize treatment options, select appropriate methods for initiating labor, and coordinate patient care within the overall framework of obstetric management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 754
retrieved_docs.content: tissue disorder categories, which in turn relate to general labor‑and‑delivery care indications. The umbrella of “Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother” integrates distinct clinical outcomes—postpartum hemorrhage, fetal death, cervical laceration, and pelvic hematoma—demonstrating the interconnectedness of maternal tissue pathology, obstetric injuries, and the need for comprehensive postpartum management. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in linking anatomical abnormalities to potential obstetric complications and in selecting appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies during labor, delivery, and the puerperium.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1051
retrieved_docs.content: The domain of LABOR AND PARTURITION encompasses the obstetric condition referred to in Arabic as مخاض مهدد (threatened labor). Early or threatened labor is classified as مخاض مهدد, indicating that this specific presentation falls within the broader category of threatened labor under the umbrella of LABOR AND PARTURITION. Understanding that مخاض مهدد is a distinct subset of labor and parturition aids clinicians in recognizing early signs of labor complications and guides appropriate monitoring and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that influence the management of the mother, and it specifically encompasses the sub‑condition of cervical shortening occurring in the second trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: constitute major complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. Recognizing the placement of each specific condition within its broader category supports comprehensive assessment, risk stratification, and coordinated management of pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2656
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother is a clinical category that encompasses both تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم (obstetric cervical tear) and ورم دموي توليدي حوضي (obstetric pelvic hematoma). This category is also associated with the adverse outcome موت الجنين (fetal death), indicating that cervical tears and pelvic hematomas occurring in the postpartum period can contribute to fetal mortality and require careful management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2927
retrieved_docs.content: Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery are classified as a subset of other procedures to assist delivery, indicating that these specific interventions are encompassed within the broader category of techniques used to facilitate and support the childbirth process.
